515	\section{The dimension-shift step}
516	
517	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
518	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
519	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
520	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
521	complex-level homology long exact sequence
522	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
523	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We first record the shift at the
524	\emph{resolution} level — where the horseshoe lift directly supplies the
525	degreewise-split short exact sequence of injective resolutions — and then specialize
526	to objects.
527	
528	\begin{lemma}[Resolution-level dimension shift]
529	  \label{lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
530	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES, CategoryTheory.shortExact_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.shortExact_map_mapHomologicalComplex_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_homology_mapHomologicalComplex_of_isRightAcyclic}
531	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
532	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
533	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
534	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\) be a short exact sequence with \(J\) right-\(G\)-acyclic,
535	  lifted to a \emph{degreewise-split} short exact sequence of injective resolutions
536	  \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\) (chain maps \(\varphi, \psi\) with
537	  \(\psi \circ \varphi = 0\) and a splitting of \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\)
538	  in each degree \(n\)). Then for every \(k \geq 0\) the connecting map of the homology
539	  long exact sequence of \(G(I_\bullet)\) is an isomorphism
540	  \[
541	    (R^{k+1} G)(Z) \;\cong\; (R^{k+2} G)(A).
542	  \]
543	\end{lemma}
544	
545	\begin{proof}
546	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
547	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
548	  Applying \(G\) degreewise to the degreewise-split sequence preserves each split
549	  degree, yielding a short exact sequence of cochain complexes
550	  \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). Its homology long
551	  exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) has connecting maps
552	  \(\delta^m : H^m(G(I_Z)) \to H^{m+1}(G(I_A))\); transport these across the
553	  injective-resolution computation \(H^m(G(I_-)) \cong (R^m G)(-)\)
554	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). For \(m = k+1 \geq 1\) the two
555	  homology objects flanking \(\delta^m\) in the long exact sequence both sit on the
556	  middle complex \(G(I_J)\), namely \(H^{k+1}(G(I_J))\) and \(H^{k+2}(G(I_J))\); both
557	  vanish because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}).
558	  With both neighbours zero, \(\delta^{k+1}\) is an isomorphism, giving
559	  \((R^{k+1} G)(Z) \cong (R^{k+2} G)(A)\).
560	\end{proof}
561	
562	\begin{lemma}\leanok
563	[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
564	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
565	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
566	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
567	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
568	    lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
569	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
570	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
571	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
572	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
573	  % between abelian categories and assume
574	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
575	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
576	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
577	  % \begin{enumerate}
578	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
579	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
580	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
581	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
582	  % \end{enumerate}
583	  % In each of the three cases
584	  % $$
585	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
586	  % $$
587	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
588	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
589	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
590	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
591	  \[
592	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
593	  \]
594	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
595	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of
596	  right-derived functors furnish isomorphisms
597	  \[
598	    (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
599	    \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
600	  \]
601	  (The companion lowest-degree statement
602	  \((R^{1} G)(A) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J) \to G(Z))\) is isolated as the
603	  sibling Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
604	\end{lemma}
605	
606	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
607	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
608	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
609	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
610	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
611	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
612	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
613	\begin{proof}
614	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
615	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
616	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
617	    \leanok
618	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
619	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
620	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
621	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
622	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
623	
624	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
625	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
626	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
627	  complexes
628	  \[
629	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
630	  \]
631	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
632	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
633	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
634	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
635	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
636	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
637	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
638	  \[
639	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
640	      \longrightarrow 0
641	  \]
642	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
643	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
644	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
645	  \[
646	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
647	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
648	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
649	  \]
650	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
651	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
652	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
653	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
654	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
655	  sequence of right-derived functors
656	  \[
657	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
658	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
659	  \]
660	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
661	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
662	  \[
663	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
664	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
665	  \]
666	
667	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
668	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
669	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
670	  \[
671	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
672	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
673	  \]
674	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
675	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism, which is the
676	  asserted dimension shift. (The lowest-degree segment of the same long exact
677	  sequence yields the cokernel description of \((R^1 G)(A)\); that reading is
678	  recorded separately in Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
679	\end{proof}
680	
681	\begin{lemma}\leanok
682	[Lowest-degree cokernel description across an acyclic short exact sequence]
683	  \label{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}
684	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedOneIsoCokerOfAcyclic}
685	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
686	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
687	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
688	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
689	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
690	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
691	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
692	  % between abelian categories and assume
693	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
694	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
695	  % of $\mathcal{A}$."
696	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "As $RF$ is an exact functor and since
697	  % $R^iF = 0$ for $i < 0$ and $R^0F = F$
698	  % (Lemma \ref{lemma-left-exact-higher-derived})
699	  % we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
700	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
701	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
702	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
703	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
704	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
705	  \[
706	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
707	  \]
708	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
709	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
710	  \[
711	    (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
712	  \]
713	  i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by the
714	  surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).
715	\end{lemma}
716	
717	\begin{proof}
718	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
719	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
720	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
721	    \leanok
722	  This reads off the lowest-degree segment of the very long exact sequence of
723	  right-derived functors built, from the same short exact sequence
724	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\), in the proof of
725	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}; here we use only its initial terms, so we
726	  recall the construction briefly and then specialize. By the horseshoe lift
727	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
728	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a degreewise-split short exact
729	  sequence of cochain complexes \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\). Applying the
730	  additive functor \(G\) degreewise preserves each split degree, giving a short exact
731	  sequence of complexes \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in
732	  \(\mathcal{B}\); its complex-level homology long exact sequence
733	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}), transported across the
734	  injective-resolution computation \(H^k(G(I_\bullet)) \cong (R^k G)(-)\)
735	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}) and using
736	  \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}), has low-degree
737	  initial segment
738	  \[
739	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J).
740	  \]
741	  Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), the right
742	  end \((R^1 G)(J)\) vanishes. Hence the segment
743	  \(G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to 0\) is exact with vanishing
744	  right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is the image of
745	  \(G(J) \to G(Z)\). Therefore \(\delta^0\) factors through an isomorphism
746	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \cong\ } (R^1 G)(A)\),
747	  which is the assertion.

750	\section{Cosyzygies of an acyclic resolution}
751	
752	The comparison theorem decomposes an acyclic resolution into the short exact
753	sequences cut out by its cosyzygies, and reads the cohomology of the applied complex
754	off those sequences using left-exactness of \(G\). We isolate the two ingredients
755	here.
756	
757	\begin{lemma}[Cosyzygy short exact sequences of an acyclic resolution]
758	  \label{lem:cosyzygy_ses}
759	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortExact, CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex, CategoryTheory.cosyzygyKernelFork, CategoryTheory.cosyzygy_iCycles_comp_toCycles, CategoryTheory.epi_toCycles_of_exactAt}
760	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
761	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
762	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
763	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838).
764	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
765	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
766	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
767	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
768	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
769	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
770	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
771	  \[
772	    0 \longrightarrow A
773	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
774	      \longrightarrow \cdots
775	  \]
776	  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
777	  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
778	  \[
779	    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
780	      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
781	  \]
782	  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
783	  \[
784	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
785	      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
786	  \]
787	  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
788	  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
789	  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.
790	  In particular \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\).
791	\end{lemma}
792	
793	\begin{proof}
794	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
795	  Fix \(m \geq 0\). Mono of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) is immediate: \(Z^m\) is by
796	  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
797	  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
798	  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
799	  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
800	  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):
801	  the image of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) equals \(\ker(d^m)\) --- for \(m \geq 1\)
802	  by the definition \(Z^m = \ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\), and for \(m = 0\) because
803	  exactness of \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to J^1\) makes \(A \hookrightarrow J^0\) identify
804	  \(A\) with \(\ker(d^0)\) --- while the kernel of the epimorphism
805	  \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) is exactly \(\ker(d^m)\). Thus
806	  \(\operatorname{im}(Z^m \to J^m) = \ker(J^m \to Z^{m+1})\), so \((S_m)\) is short
807	  exact. The middle term \(J^m\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis. The final
808	  identity \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\)
809	  is the case \(m = 0\): \(\operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0) = \operatorname{coker}(Z^0 \to J^0)
810	  \cong Z^1\) by \((S_0)\).
811	\end{proof}

814	  \label{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}

825	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}), left-exact initial segment.}

827	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, for every \(n \geq 0\) the morphism \(G\) applies to
828	  the inclusion \(Z^n \hookrightarrow J^n\) to give an isomorphism

831	  \]

839	  Fix \(n \geq 0\). Apply the left-exact functor \(G\) to the cosyzygy short exact
840	  sequence \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\) of
841	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}; left-exactness yields an exact sequence
842	  \[
843	    0 \longrightarrow G(Z^n) \longrightarrow G(J^n) \longrightarrow G(Z^{n+1}),
844	  \]
845	  so \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) is the kernel of \(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})\).
846	  Next, the differential \(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\) of the applied complex factors as
847	  \(G(J^n) \xrightarrow{G(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1})} G(Z^{n+1})
848	  \xrightarrow{G(Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1})} G(J^{n+1})\), because the original
849	  differential \(J^n \to J^{n+1}\) factors through its image \(Z^{n+1}\) as
850	  \(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1}\) and \(G\) preserves
851	  composition. The second factor \(G(Z^{n+1}) \to G(J^{n+1})\) is a monomorphism (the
852	  image of the left-exact application of \(G\) to \((S_{n+1})\), whose first map
853	  \(G(Z^{n+1}) \hookrightarrow G(J^{n+1})\) is mono). A monomorphism does not change
854	  kernels under precomposition, so
855	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) = \ker(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})) = G(Z^n)\). Hence
856	  \(G(Z^n) \xrightarrow{\cong} \ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\) as subobjects of
857	  \(G(J^n)\). For \(n = 0\), \(Z^0 = A\) gives
858	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0(G(J^\bullet))\), the last equality being
859	  the definition of the zeroth cohomology of a cochain complex starting in degree
860	  \(0\) (no incoming differential). The use of \(R^0 G \cong G\)
861	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) is what licenses reading \(G(A)\) as
862	  \((R^0 G)(A)\) when this identification feeds the comparison theorem.
863	\end{proof}

865	\begin{lemma}[Cohomology of the applied resolution as a cosyzygy cokernel]
866	  \label{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution}

873	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
874	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA) and Lemma
875	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
876	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838 and L5619--5654).
877	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
878	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences

880	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
881	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
882	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}) and Tag 015D
883	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
884	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
885	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, the cohomology of the applied complex
886	  \(G(J^\bullet) = \bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to \cdots\bigr)\) is computed by
887	  \[
888	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\; G(A),
889	    \qquad
890	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\;
891	      \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr) \quad (n \geq 1),
892	  \]
893	  where \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) is \(G\) applied to the surjection
894	  \(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n\) of \((S_{n-1})\).

897	\begin{proof}
898	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
899	  The degree-zero statement is the \(n = 0\) case of
900	  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
901	  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
902	  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
903	  \[
904	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
905	      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
906	            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).
907	  \]
908	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles} the numerator is
909	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
910	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
911	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
912	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)
913	  \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) by the factorization of \(J^{n-1} \to J^n\) through its image
914	  \(Z^n\), so its image equals the image of \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) inside the
915	  subobject \(G(Z^n)\). Therefore the quotient
916	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n))\) is exactly
917	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
918	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
919	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).
920	\end{proof}

961	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
962	  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.

1004	  infrastructure and the dimension-shift step proved above. Cut the resolution into
1005	  its cosyzygy short exact sequences \((S_m) : 0 \to Z^m \to J^m \to Z^{m+1} \to 0\)
1006	  with \(Z^0 = A\) and acyclic middle terms, as furnished by
1007	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}.

1010	  \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) \cong G(A)\), and by \(R^0 G \cong G\)
1011	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) the right-hand side is \((R^0 G)(A)\).
1012	  This is the case \(n = 0\).
1013

1015	  \((S_0), \ldots, (S_{n-2})\) all have acyclic middle terms, so the dimension-shift
1016	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} applied to them gives, for each \(k \geq 1\),
1017	  isomorphisms \((R^k G)(Z^{m+1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(Z^m)\) for \(0 \le m \le n-2\)
1018	  (with \(Z^0 = A\)). Composing them down the staircase
1019	  \[
1020	    (R^n G)(A)
1021	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
1022	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
1023	      \;\cong\; \cdots
1024	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1})

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, definition of $R^i f_*$ as the
5	% $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex
6	% and references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter records the derived-functor higher direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\)
11	for \(i \geq 1\). It provides the single object that the {\v C}ech computation of
12	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} compares against: the comparison
13	theorem \(\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}\) identifies the cohomology of the
14	relative {\v C}ech complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) wherever the derived functor
15	is defined.
16	
17	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category).
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
19	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	
21	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
22	
23	\begin{definition}\leanok
24	[Higher direct image]
25	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
26	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
27	  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
28	  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has
29	  % `Abelian X.Modules` but NOT `EnoughInjectives` / `IsGrothendieckAbelian` for
30	  % `SheafOfModules` over a sheaf of rings (the Grothendieck-abelian instance is
31	  % only for `Sheaf J A` with a fixed value category). The definition is therefore
32	  % conditional; the hypothesis can be dropped once a lane proves that instance.
33	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
34	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
35	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
36	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
37	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
38	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
39	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
40	  % the sheaf
41	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
42	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
43	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
44	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
45	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
46	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
47	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
48	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
49	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
50	  sheafification of the presheaf
51	  \[
52	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
53	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
54	  \]
55	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
56	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
57	\end{definition}

21	\section{Setup and motivation}
22	
23	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
24	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
25	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
26	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
27	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
28	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
29	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
30	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
31	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
32	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
33	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
34	
35	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
36	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
37	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
38	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
39	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
40	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
41	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
42	\[
43	  \begin{array}{ccc}
44	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
45	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
46	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
47	  \end{array}
48	\]
49	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
50	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
51	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
52	
53	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
54	
55	\begin{definition}
56	
57	\leanok
58	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
59	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
61	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
62	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
63	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
64	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
65	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
66	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
67	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
68	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
69	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
70	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
71	  % the unit ideal, see
72	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
73	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
74	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
75	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
76	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
77	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
78	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
79	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
80	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
81	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
82	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
83	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
84	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
85	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
86	  \[
87	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
88	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
89	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
90	  \]
91	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
92	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
93	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
94	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
95	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
96	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
97	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
98	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
99	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
100	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
101	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
102	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
103	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
104	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
105	\end{definition}
106	
107	\begin{definition}
108	
109	\leanok
110	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
111	  \label{def:cech_complex}
112	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
113	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
114	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
115	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
116	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
118	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
119	  % is identified with the complex
120	  % $$
121	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
122	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
123	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
124	  % \ldots
125	  % $$
126	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
127	  % \begin{equation}
128	  % \label{equation-extended}
129	  % 0 \to
130	  % M \to
131	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
132	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
133	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
134	  % \ldots
135	  % \end{equation}
136	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
137	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
138	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
139	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
140	  vanishing).}
141	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
142	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
143	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
144	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
145	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
146	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
147	  in degree \(p\) it is
148	  \[
149	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
150	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
151	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
152	  \]
153	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
154	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
155	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
156	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
157	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
158	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
159	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
160	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
161	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
162	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
163	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
164	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
165	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
166	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
167	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
168	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
169	\end{definition}
170	
171	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
172	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
173	
174	It is convenient to factor the construction of
175	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
176	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
177	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
178	
179	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
180	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
181	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
182	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
183	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
184	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
185	\[
186	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
187	    \;=\;
188	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
189	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
190	\]
191	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
192	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
193	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
194	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
195	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
196	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
197	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
198	
199	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
200	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by

input.description: Run leandag build to check dependency graph

content: Let me continue reading the main consolidated chapter and also get more of the leandag output.

200	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
201	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
202	\[
203	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
204	  \qquad
205	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
206	\]
207	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
208	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
209	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
210	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
211	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
212	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
213	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
214	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
215	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
216	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
217	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
218	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
219	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
220	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
221	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
222	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
223	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
224	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
225	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
226	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
227	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
228	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
229	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
230	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
231	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
232	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
233	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
234	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
235	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
236	
237	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
238	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
239	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
240	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
241	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
242	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
243	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
244	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
245	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
246	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
247	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
248	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
249	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
250	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
251	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
252	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
253	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
254	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
255	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
256	
257	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
258	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
259	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
260	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
261	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
262	
263	\begin{definition}\leanok
264	[Arrow of an affine cover]
265	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
266	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
267	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
268	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
269	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
270	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
271	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
272	  apply.
273	\end{definition}
274	
275	\begin{definition}[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
276	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
277	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOver, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOverAug}
278	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
279	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
280	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
281	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
282	  \[
283	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
284	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
285	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
286	  \]
287	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
288	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
289	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
290	\end{definition}
291	
292	\begin{definition}\leanok
293	[Push--pull object map]
294	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
295	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
296	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
297	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
298	  \[
299	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
300	  \]
301	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
302	\end{definition}
303	
304	\begin{definition}[Push--pull comparison map]
305	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
306	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_eq_raw, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self_gen, AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardComp_hom_app_id, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_comp}
307	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
308	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
309	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
310	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
311	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
312	  \[
313	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
314	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
315	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
316	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
317	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
318	      \;=\;
319	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
320	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
321	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
322	  \]
323	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
324	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
325	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
326	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
327	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
328	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
329	\end{definition}
330	
331	\begin{lemma}
332	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
333	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
334	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
335	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
336	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
337	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
338	\end{lemma}
339	
340	\begin{proof}
341	  A direct calculation with comparison isomorphisms, with no sectionwise content.
342	  Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward comparison as the
343	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison; the identity leg then collapses
344	  by the identity normalisation of the pseudofunctor together with right unitality,
345	  and the two over-triangle transport coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
346	\end{proof}
347	
348	\begin{lemma}
349	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
350	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
351	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_pentagon}
352	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
353	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
354	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
355	  \[
356	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
357	  \]
358	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
359	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
360	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
361	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
362	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
363	\end{lemma}
364	
365	\begin{proof}
366	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
367	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
368	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
369	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
370	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
371	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
372	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
373	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
374	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
375	\end{proof}
376	
377	\begin{lemma}
378	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
379	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
380	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
381	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
382	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
383	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
384	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
385	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
386	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
387	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
388	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
389	  \[
390	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
391	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
392	    \;=\;
393	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
394	  \]
395	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
396	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
397	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
398	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
399	\end{lemma}
400	
401	\begin{proof}
402	  A direct mate-calculus computation: unfold both sides using the definition of
403	  the adjunction unit for the composite and the pseudofunctor structure maps, then
404	  verify the equality by the mate identity for the pushforward/pullback comparison.
405	\end{proof}

407	\begin{lemma}
408	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
409	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
410	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}
411	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
412	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the
413	  over-triangle \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\), and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
414	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
415	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
416	  collapses to the transport-light form
417	  \[
418	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
419	      \;\circ\; e_2
420	    \;=\;
421	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
422	      \;\circ\; e_3,
423	  \]
424	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by
425	  \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\). It states the over-triangle cancellation in the
426	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} with the
427	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
428	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
429	  composition (pentagon) law.
430	\end{lemma}
431	
432	\begin{proof}
433	  Substitute the over-triangle equality \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\) to replace
434	  transport coercions by identities, then verify the cancellation by direct
435	  calculation with the pushforward comparison.
436	\end{proof}

513	\subsection{The categorical--module bridge (L1)}
514	\label{sec:cech_l1_bridge}
515	
516	The affine vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is a statement about the
517	\emph{section} {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
518	\mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- a cochain complex of
519	abelian groups --- whereas the combinatorial contracting-homotopy core operates on a
520	concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules. The passage between the two, the
521	\emph{categorical--module bridge}, decomposes into two self-contained statements: the
522	section identification (each section group is an away localisation, with the
523	restriction maps the canonical localisation maps) and the homology-to-exactness
524	reduction (vanishing of the abstract homology is equivalent to exactness of the
525	underlying localised-module complex). These are isolated as
526	Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and
527	Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} below; the assembly --- feeding each
528	positive-degree node through the local-to-global exactness criterion and closing with
529	the dependent combinatorial port --- is carried out in the proof of
530	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.

735	  \uses{def:qcoh_sections_localized, def:section_cech_complex}
736	  The term identification is the degreewise statement of
737	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: the degree-\(p\) section group
738	  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is \(\prod_\sigma M_{s_\sigma}\), since
739	  each \((p+1)\)-fold intersection \(D(s_{\sigma(0)}) \cap \cdots \cap
740	  D(s_{\sigma(p)})\) is the basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product
741	  \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\) and the sections there are the away
742	  localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). The differential identification is item~(5) of
743	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: each face restriction
744	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})) \to \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is the
745	  canonical localisation map \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\), so the
746	  alternating sum of restrictions is the alternating sum of localisation maps, the
747	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) (the coface match of
748	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). The homology vanishing then follows in
749	  three steps. First, \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\) turns
750	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology at \(p\) into \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) of
751	  the short complex at \(p\). Second, because the degree-\(p\) objects are
752	  \emph{categorical products in \(\mathrm{Ab}\)} rather than \(R\)-modules, the abelian
753	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
754	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}) --- not the module-category criterion ---
755	  reduces \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) to the underlying-group exactness
756	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\). Third, the
757	  element-level product equivalence
758	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
759	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
760	  identifies each product group with the per-\(\sigma\) localised module \(D^p\), so the
761	  group-level exactness is exactly \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to
762	  D^{p+1})\), supplied by \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\)
763	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}).
764	
765	  The identification of the abstract section complex with the concrete localised
766	  complex \(D^\bullet\) --- together with the construction of the prepend transport
767	  \(c\) and the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\) discharging the unit, shift, and
768	  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\)/\(\mathtt{hsh}\)/\(\mathtt{hcomm}\)
769	  of the dependent combinatorial port --- is the bulk of the bridge. The non-circular
770	  route is reflection along the localisation equivalence on its essential image (the

846	  \(i \in \mathrm{Fin}(q+2)\), write
847	  \[

849	      \mathcal{F}\Bigl(\,\bigcap_{l} U_{(\sigma \circ d_i)(l)}\Bigr)

856	  obtained from the order-preserving coface \(d_i\) of the simplex category. Then,
857	  read through the product equivalence

859	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}, the underlying group action of the
860	  degree-\(q\) differential

875	  abstract shape that the localisation differential \(\operatorname{dDiff}\) of
876	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} matches once the tilde-bridge

884	  is the alternating sum \(\sum_{i} (-1)^i\, d^i\) of the cosimplicial cofaces, each
885	  coface \(d^i\) being the \(\operatorname{Pi.lift}\) whose \(\sigma\)-component

890	  past the categorical sum: applying the sum of \(\mathrm{Ab}\)-morphisms to an element

1345	\subsection{Presheaf-level {\v C}ech machinery}
1346	\label{sec:presheaf_cech_machinery}
1347	
1348	The {\v C}ech-acyclicity of injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1349	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single
1350	affine cover: it is proved on the abelian category \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
1351	of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, where the {\v C}ech complex of sections
1352	is the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex of an explicit free resolution. The argument
1353	rests on two presheaf-level facts, recorded here, and one transfer of injectivity.
1354	First, a free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves of modules whose
1355	\(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex into \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{is} the {\v C}ech complex
1356	of sections (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
1357	Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and
1358	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}). Second, the resolution property of
1359	that complex: \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) resolves \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\)
1360	concentrated in degree \(0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}). Together
1361	these give: an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is injective as a presheaf
1362	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} via
1363	Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} applied to the sheafification adjunction
1364	Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), so \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) is
1365	exact and carries the augmented resolution to an exact section complex, i.e.\
1366	\(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\). This direct route uses
1367	neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
1368	\(\delta\)-functor identification.

1414	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
1415	  open covering, with \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\)
1416	  the open immersion of each finite intersection. The Stacks formulation uses, for
1417	  each intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), the \emph{extension-by-zero} presheaf
1418	  \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\), the presheaf of
1419	  modules sending an open \(W\) to \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when
1420	  \(W \subset U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) and to \(0\) otherwise. This extension-by-zero
1421	  presheaf is, canonically, the \emph{free presheaf of modules on the representable}
1422	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\): writing
1423	  \(\mathbf{y}\,V = \operatorname{Hom}(-, V)\) for the Yoneda presheaf of the open
1424	  \(V\) on the site of opens of \(X\), the free presheaf of modules
1425	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V)\) sends \(W\) to the free
1426	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\)-module on the set \(\operatorname{Hom}(W, V)\), which is
1427	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) if \(W \subset V\) and \(0\) otherwise — exactly
1428	  extension-by-zero of \(\mathcal{O}_X|_V\). Its defining universal property
1429	  \[

1431	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V),\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1432	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{F}(V)
1433	  \]
1434	  is the free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules composed with the
1435	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, G) \cong G(V)\). The
1436	  \emph{free presheaf complex} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the chain complex
1437	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) in the abelian category
1438	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose
1439	  term in degree \(p\) is the direct sum

1441	    K(\mathcal{U})_p

3510	\begin{lemma}[The underlying-abelian-sheaf functor preserves finite colimits]
3511	  \label{lem:toSheaf_preservesFiniteColimits}
3512	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.toSheaf_preservesFiniteColimits}
3513	  \uses{lem:sheafificationCompToSheaf_mathlib, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}
3514	  \textit{Project-bespoke gap-fill (the missing right-exact dual of \(\mathrm{toSheaf}\)).}
3515	  The forgetful functor \(\mathrm{toSheaf}\,R : \mathrm{Mod}(R) \to \mathrm{Sh}(J, \mathbf{Ab})\)
3516	  from sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to the underlying sheaves of abelian groups preserves
3517	  finite colimits. This is the right-exact counterpart of the limit-side fact that
3518	  \(\mathrm{toSheaf}\,R\) preserves finite limits; Mathlib supplies the latter but not the former, so
3519	  it is built here.
3520	\end{lemma}
3521	\begin{proof}
3522	  \uses{lem:sheafificationCompToSheaf_mathlib, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}
3523	  Write \(L := \operatorname{sheafification}(\mathrm{id}_{R_0}) : \mathrm{PreMod}(R_0) \to
3524	  \mathrm{Mod}(R)\) for the module-sheafification functor; \(L\) is a left adjoint whose counit is an
3525	  isomorphism, so it is a localization that preserves all colimits. Throughout, \(\circ\) denotes
3526	  diagrammatic composition: \(F \circ G\) means ``apply \(F\), then \(G\)''.
3527	
3528	  \emph{Step 1 (the composite \(L \circ \mathrm{toSheaf}\) preserves finite colimits).} By the
3529	  square isomorphism (Lemma~\ref{lem:sheafificationCompToSheaf_mathlib}),
3530	  \(L \circ \mathrm{toSheaf}\,R \cong \mathrm{toPresheaf}\,R_0 \circ
3531	  \operatorname{sheafify}_J^{\mathrm{Ab}}\). The forgetful functor \(\mathrm{toPresheaf}\,R_0\) on
3532	  presheaves of modules preserves finite colimits (colimits of presheaves of modules are computed
3533	  objectwise on the underlying abelian groups), and abelian-group sheafification
3534	  \(\operatorname{sheafify}_J^{\mathrm{Ab}}\) is a left adjoint, hence preserves all colimits.
3535	  Therefore their composite, and so the isomorphic functor \(L \circ \mathrm{toSheaf}\,R\), preserves
3536	  finite colimits.
3537	
3538	  \emph{Step 2 (descend from the composite to \(\mathrm{toSheaf}\,R\) alone).} Let
3539	  \(\iota : \mathrm{Mod}(R) \hookrightarrow \mathrm{PreMod}(R_0)\) be the inclusion of sheaves of
3540	  modules into presheaves of modules, so that \(L \dashv \iota\) is the sheafification adjunction
3541	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}). The descent proceeds in three clauses.
3542	
3543	  \emph{(i) The counit is an isomorphism.} Every sheaf of modules is already a sheaf, so its
3544	  sheafification recovers it: the counit of \(L \dashv \iota\) is a natural isomorphism
3545	  \[
3546	    \varepsilon : \iota \circ L \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathrm{Mod}(R)} .
3547	  \]
3548	
3549	  \emph{(ii) \(\mathrm{toSheaf}\,R\) is a retract of \(L \circ \mathrm{toSheaf}\,R\).} Whiskering the
3550	  counit isomorphism \(\varepsilon\) with \(\mathrm{toSheaf}\,R\) yields a natural isomorphism
3551	  \[
3552	    \mathrm{toSheaf}\,R \;\cong\; \iota \circ \bigl(L \circ \mathrm{toSheaf}\,R\bigr),
3553	  \]
3554	  which exhibits \(\mathrm{toSheaf}\,R\) as a retract of the composite \(L \circ \mathrm{toSheaf}\,R\):
3555	  the section is precomposition with \(\iota\), and the retraction is \(\varepsilon\) whiskered with
3556	  \(\mathrm{toSheaf}\,R\). By Step 1 the composite \(L \circ \mathrm{toSheaf}\,R\) preserves finite
3557	  colimits.

3559	  \emph{(iii) A retract of a finite-colimit-preserving functor preserves finite colimits.} If \(G\)
3560	  sends a finite colimit cocone to a colimit cocone and \(F\) is a retract of \(G\), then the colimit
3561	  comparison map for \(F\) is a retract of the (isomorphism) comparison map for \(G\), hence itself an
3562	  isomorphism, so \(F\) preserves that colimit. Applying this with \(F = \mathrm{toSheaf}\,R\) and
3563	  \(G = L \circ \mathrm{toSheaf}\,R\), we conclude that \(\mathrm{toSheaf}\,R\) preserves finite
3564	  colimits.
3565	\end{proof}

3877	  % $x \in U \subset X$ such that $\mathcal{F}|_U$ is isomorphic to the quasi-coherent
3878	  % sheaf associated to some $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module $M$. ... Now Algebra, Lemma
3879	  % \ref{algebra-lemma-glue-modules} shows that there exist an $R$-module $M$ such that
3880	  % $M_i = M_{f_i}$ compatible with the morphisms $\psi_{ij}$. ... By construction this
3881	  % map reduces to the isomorphisms $\varphi_i^{-1}$ on each $D(f_i)$ and hence is an
3882	  % isomorphism."

3884	  (proof).}
3885	  The unconditional quasi-coherent statement
3886	  \([{\,}\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\,\mathcal{F}{\,}] \Rightarrow \mathcal{F} \cong
3887	  \widetilde{\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})}\) reduces, through
3888	  Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections}, to the single instance
3889	  \([{\,}\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\,\mathcal{F}{\,}] \Rightarrow
3890	  \operatorname{IsIso}(\operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma})\) on the affine base \(\operatorname{Spec} R\).
3891	  This is the content of Stacks Tag 01I8, organised here as a three-step route into the
3892	  presentation-form discharge:
3893	  \begin{enumerate}
3894	  \item \emph{Global generation.} A quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) is
3895	    generated by its global sections: one produces a global epimorphism
3896	    \(\mathcal{O}_X^{(I)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Quasi-coherence gives generators only
3897	    locally on a basic-open cover; these are globalised using the quasi-compactness of
3898	    \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) and the localisation-of-sections property of quasi-coherent sheaves
3899	    (sections over \(D(f)\) are the away localisation), the affine global-generation statement of
3900	    Tag 01I8. This is realised by the finite basic-open subcover
3901	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover}, P0), the localised-sections core
3902	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_localized_sections}, P1), and their assembly into a global generating
3903	    family (Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_global_generation}, P2).
3904	  \item \emph{Presentation.} The kernel of \(\mathcal{O}_X^{(I)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is
3905	    again quasi-coherent on \(\operatorname{Spec} R\), hence again globally generated by step (1);
3906	    composing yields a global presentation \(\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{Presentation}\). The kernel's
3907	    quasi-coherence uses the exactness of \(\widetilde{(-)}\)
3908	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:tilde_preserves_kernels}) to land at Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_kernel_qcoh} (P3); the
3909	    two generating families \(\sigma, \tau\) are assembled into the counit-iso input by
3910	    Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_fromTildeGamma_of_genSections}.
3911	  \item \emph{Counit iso.} Feeding that presentation to
3912	    Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_fromTildeGamma_of_presentation} makes
3913	    \(\operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma}\) an isomorphism; equivalently, this is the discharge of
3914	    Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections_of_presentation}. Packaged as the unconditional
3915	    instance, this is Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_fromTildeGamma_of_quasicoherent} (P4), which upgrades
3916	    Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections} to its unconditional quasi-coherent form through
3917	    Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections_of_genSections}.
3918	  \end{enumerate}
3919	  Step (1) is the load-bearing affine global-generation input; steps (2)--(3) are formal. The same
3920	  route is recorded as the route to the affine equivalence \(\operatorname{QCoh}(\operatorname{Spec}
3921	  R) \simeq \operatorname{Mod}(R)\) (Tag 01I8, \texttt{lemma-equivalence-quasi-coherent}).
3922	
3923	  The chain deliberately takes this \emph{global-generation} route (Hartshorne~II.5.14--17, Stacks
3924	  Tag 01I8) rather than the alternative \emph{module-gluing} route suggested by the Stacks proof
3925	  above (which constructs \(M\) by gluing the local modules \(M_i\) along the cocycle
3926	  \(\psi_{ij}\), Algebra \texttt{lemma-glue-modules}). The module-gluing route is not pursued here:
3927	  it requires gluing data of modules and the effective faithfully-flat descent equivalence, neither
3928	  of which is available in the ambient library, whereas every step of the global-generation route
3929	  reduces to ordinary commutative-algebra and sheaf statements (localisation of sections, finite
3930	  quasi-compact patching, locality of kernels) that land directly on the presentation-form discharge
3931	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_fromTildeGamma_of_presentation}. The named blocks
3932	  Lemma~\ref{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover} (P0),
3933	  Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_localized_sections} (P1),
3934	  Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_global_generation} (P2),
3935	  Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_kernel_qcoh} (P3), and
3936	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_fromTildeGamma_of_quasicoherent} (P4)
3937	  below carry out the route P0--P4.
3938	\end{remark}
3939	
3940	The five blocks below carry out the global-generation route P0--P4 of the decomposition
3941	remark, together with the two generating-family assembly helpers and the free-module
3942	quasi-coherence fact they consume. All are anchored to the Stacks Project section
3943	``Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines''.

3945	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3946	[Free \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules are quasi-coherent]
3947	  \label{lem:free_isQuasicoherent}
3948	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.free_isQuasicoherent}
3949	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma
3950	  % \texttt{lemma-colimit-quasi-coherent} (read from
3951	  % references/stacks-schemes.tex, L1434--1438).
3952	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The direct sum of an arbitrary collection of quasi-coherent sheaves
3953	  % on $X$ is quasi-coherent. The same holds for colimits."
3954	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-colimit-quasi-coherent}.}
3955	  Let \(X = \operatorname{Spec} R\). For any index type \(\iota\) the free
3956	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{O}_X^{(\iota)}\) on \(\iota\) generators is
3957	  quasi-coherent. Indeed it is the sheaf \(\widetilde{R^{(\iota)}}\) associated to the
3958	  free \(R\)-module \(R^{(\iota)} = \bigoplus_{\iota} R\) (the finitely-supported
3959	  functions \(\iota \to R\)): the tilde functor carries \(R^{(\iota)}\) to a direct sum
3960	  of copies of the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X = \widetilde{R}\), and every sheaf of
3961	  the form \(\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. Quasi-coherence is preserved under the
3962	  isomorphism \(\mathcal{O}_X^{(\iota)} \cong \widetilde{R^{(\iota)}}\); concretely it is
3963	  obtained from the identification of the free \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module with the
3964	  tilde of the free \(R\)-module.
3965	\end{lemma}

3967	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3968	[Finite basic-open refinement of a cover of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\)]
3969	  \label{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover}
3970	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover}
3971	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, \texttt{lemma-standard-open} (parts 2--3) and
3972	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine} (proof) (read from
3973	  % references/stacks-schemes.tex, L531--534, L1289--1301).
3974	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item Any open covering of $D(f)$ can be refined to a finite
3975	  % open covering of the form $D(f) = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(g_i)$.
3976	  % \item If $g_1, \ldots, g_n \in R$, then $D(f) \subset \bigcup D(g_i)$
3977	  % if and only if $g_1, \ldots, g_n$ generate the unit ideal in $R_f$."
3978	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Since every standard open $D(f)$ is quasi-compact we see that
3979	  % $X$ is locally quasi-compact, i.e., every point has a fundamental
3980	  % system of quasi-compact neighbourhoods ...
3981	  % In other words, we get an open covering by $U$'s with this property.

3984	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-standard-open} and
3985	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine} (proof).}
3986	  Let \(R\) be a ring and \((U_i)_{i \in \iota}\) a family of opens of
3987	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) with \(\bigsqcup_i U_i = \operatorname{Spec} R\) (i.e.\
3988	  \(\bigcup_i U_i = \top\)). Then there exist finitely many elements
3989	  \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in R\) and indices \(\varphi : \{1, \ldots, n\} \to \iota\) such
3990	  that each basic open satisfies \(D(f_j) \subseteq U_{\varphi(j)}\) and the \(f_j\)
3991	  generate the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{span}\{f_1, \ldots, f_n\} = R\) (equivalently
3992	  \(\bigcup_j D(f_j) = \operatorname{Spec} R\)).
3993	\end{lemma}
3994	\begin{proof}
3995	
3996	\leanok
3997	  The basic opens \(D(f)\), \(f \in R\), form a basis of the Zariski topology on
3998	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R\); hence every \(U_i\) is a union of basic opens, and refining
3999	  the cover \((U_i)\) yields a cover of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) by basic opens
4000	  \(D(f)\), each contained in some \(U_{\varphi}\). The space \(\operatorname{Spec} R\)
4001	  is quasi-compact, so finitely many of these basic opens \(D(f_1), \ldots, D(f_n)\)
4002	  already cover \(\operatorname{Spec} R\), with \(D(f_j) \subseteq U_{\varphi(j)}\) by
4003	  construction. Finally a finite family of basic opens covers all of
4004	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) if and only if the \(f_j\) generate the unit ideal:
4005	  \(\bigcup_j D(f_j) = \operatorname{Spec} R \iff \operatorname{span}\{f_j\} = R\), since
4006	  the closed complement \(V(\operatorname{span}\{f_j\})\) is empty exactly when the ideal
4007	  is the whole ring.
4008	\end{proof}

4010	\begin{lemma}[Restriction of an \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\)-module to \(D(f)\)
4011	  as an \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R_f}\)-module]
4012	  \label{lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen}
4013	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.modulesRestrictBasicOpen}
4014	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, \texttt{lemma-widetilde-pullback} and the
4015	  % remark following it, Tag 01I8 (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L1241--1276).
4016	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma \ref{lemma-widetilde-pullback} above says in particular
4017	  % that if you restrict
4018	  % the sheaf $\widetilde M$ to a standard affine open subspace
4019	  % $D(f)$, then you get $\widetilde{M_f}$. We will use this from
4020	  % now on without further mention."
4021	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-widetilde-pullback}, Tag 01I8.}
4022	  Let \(X = \operatorname{Spec} R\), let \(\mathcal{F}\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
4023	  (an object of \((\operatorname{Spec} R).\mathrm{Modules}\)), and let \(f \in R\). Then,
4024	  transporting along the canonical isomorphism of affine schemes
4025	  \(D(f) \cong \operatorname{Spec} R_f\), the restriction \(\mathcal{F}|_{D(f)}\) is naturally
4026	  an \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R_f}\)-module: there is an object
4027	  \(\mathcal{F}_{(f)} \in (\operatorname{Spec} R_f).\mathrm{Modules}\) together with an
4028	  isomorphism \(\mathcal{F}|_{D(f)} \cong \mathcal{F}_{(f)}\) of sheaves of modules over the
4029	  identification \(D(f) \cong \operatorname{Spec} R_f\). This assignment is functorial in
4030	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
4031	\end{lemma}
4032	\begin{proof}
4033	  The basic open \(D(f) \subseteq \operatorname{Spec} R\) is an affine scheme, and the
4034	  canonical comparison \(D(f) \cong \operatorname{Spec} R_f\) is an isomorphism of affine
4035	  schemes (it corresponds to the localisation ring map \(R \to R_f\)). Pulling the restricted
4036	  module \(\mathcal{F}|_{D(f)}\) back along the inverse of this isomorphism transports it to a
4037	  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R_f}\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}_{(f)}\); the unit of
4038	  the transport supplies the comparison isomorphism. Restriction of sheaves of modules and
4039	  pullback along an isomorphism are both functorial, so the assignment
4040	  \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto \mathcal{F}_{(f)}\) is functorial in \(\mathcal{F}\).
4041	\end{proof}

4164	\begin{lemma}[\(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) is local on a finite spanning cover]
4165	  \label{lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover}
4166	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover,
4167	    AlgebraicGeometry.exists_sum_pow_eq_one, AlgebraicGeometry.mem_range_of_span_pow,
4168	    AlgebraicGeometry.eq_zero_of_span_pow, AlgebraicGeometry.map_smul_endFun,
4169	    AlgebraicGeometry.bump_eq, AlgebraicGeometry.per_j_surj, AlgebraicGeometry.per_j_eq}
4170	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(M\) and \(N\) be \(R\)-modules, let
4171	  \(g : M \to N\) be an \(R\)-linear map, let \(f \in R\), and let
4172	  \(s : \{1, \ldots, n\} \to R\) be a finite family with
4173	  \(\operatorname{span}\{s_1, \ldots, s_n\} = R\) (the unit ideal). For each \(j\) write
4174	  \(M_{s_j} = (\operatorname{powers} s_j)^{-1} M\) and \(N_{s_j} = (\operatorname{powers}
4175	  s_j)^{-1} N\) for the localisations at the powers of \(s_j\), and let
4176	  \(g_{s_j} : M_{s_j} \to N_{s_j}\) be the induced \(R\)-linear (equivalently
4177	  \(R_{s_j}\)-linear) map. Suppose that for every \(j\) the localised map \(g_{s_j}\)
4178	  exhibits \(N_{s_j}\) as the localisation of \(M_{s_j}\) at the powers of (the image of)
4179	  \(f\), i.e.\ \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g_{s_j}\). Then
4180	  \(g\) itself exhibits \(N\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the powers of \(f\):
4181	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g\).
4182	\end{lemma}
4183	\begin{proof}
4184	
4185	  This is a purely commutative-algebra statement: the predicate
4186	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g\) is the conjunction of its
4187	  three defining conditions, and each is verified by descent along the spanning cover. Throughout we use the standard partition
4188	  of unity: since \(\operatorname{span}\{s_j\} = R\), for any chosen exponents \(N_j\) the
4189	  powers \(s_j^{N_j}\) again span the unit ideal, so there are \(r_j \in R\) with
4190	  \(\sum_j r_j s_j^{N_j} = 1\); and an element of an \(R\)-module is zero iff its image in
4191	  \(M_{s_j}\) vanishes for every \(j\) (locality of being zero on a spanning cover, the
4192	  \(s_j\)-torsion form of the same partition of unity).
4193	
4194	  \emph{\(f\) acts invertibly on \(N\).} For each \(j\), the hypothesis
4195	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g_{s_j}\) makes multiplication
4196	  by \(f\) on \(N_{s_j}\) bijective. Multiplication by \(f\) on \(N\) is an isomorphism iff it
4197	  is so after localising at each \(s_j\) (an \(R\)-linear endomorphism is bijective iff it is
4198	  bijective on the cover \(\{D(s_j)\}\), since injectivity and surjectivity are both local on
4199	  a spanning cover); hence \(f \cdot (-) : N \to N\) is bijective, and likewise every power
4200	  \(f^k\).
4201	
4202	  \emph{Every \(y \in N\) satisfies \(f^k y = g(x)\) for some \(x \in M\), \(k\).}
4203	  Fix \(y \in N\). For each \(j\), surjectivity of \(g_{s_j}\) as a localisation at the powers
4204	  of \(f\) yields \(m_j \in M\), exponents \(a_j\) (clearing the \(s_j\)-denominator) and
4205	  \(k_j\) with \(s_j^{a_j} f^{k_j} y = g(m_j)\) holding after localising at \(s_j\); clearing
4206	  the remaining \(s_j\)-torsion (enlarging \(a_j\)) we may take this as an honest equality
4207	  \(s_j^{a_j} f^{k_j} y = g(m_j)\) in \(N\). Choose a common \(k = \max_j k_j\) (replacing
4208	  \(m_j\) by \(f^{k - k_j} m_j\)) so that \(s_j^{a_j} f^{k} y = g(m_j)\) for all \(j\). Pick,
4209	  by the partition of unity, \(r_j\) with \(\sum_j r_j s_j^{a_j} = 1\) (possible because the
4210	  \(s_j^{a_j}\) span the unit ideal). Then
4211	  \(f^{k} y = \sum_j r_j s_j^{a_j} f^{k} y = \sum_j r_j\, g(m_j) = g\bigl(\sum_j r_j m_j\bigr)\),
4212	  so \(x = \sum_j r_j m_j\) and the exponent \(k\) work.
4213	
4214	  \emph{If \(g(x) = g(x')\) then \(f^k(x - x') = 0\) for some \(k\).} Put
4215	  \(z = x - x'\), so \(g(z) = 0\). For each \(j\), the equaliser clause of
4216	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g_{s_j}\) gives, from the
4217	  vanishing of \(g_{s_j}(z) = 0\) in \(N_{s_j}\), an exponent \(k_j\) with
4218	  \(f^{k_j} z = 0\) after localising at \(s_j\); clearing the \(s_j\)-denominator there is
4219	  \(b_j\) with \(s_j^{b_j} f^{k_j} z = 0\) in \(M\). Take \(k = \max_j k_j\), so
4220	  \(s_j^{b_j} f^{k} z = 0\) for all \(j\). With \(r_j\) chosen so that
4221	  \(\sum_j r_j s_j^{b_j} = 1\),
4222	  \(f^{k} z = \sum_j r_j s_j^{b_j} f^{k} z = 0\). Hence \(f^{k}(x - x') = 0\).
4223	
4224	  The three clauses are exactly the data of
4225	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{powers} f)\, g\).
4226	\end{proof}

4248	  Let \(X = \operatorname{Spec} R\), let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent
4249	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let \(f \in R\). Then the restriction of sections
4250	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) exhibits

4260	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_{j=1}^{n} D(s_j)\) with

4262	  \(D(s_j) = \operatorname{Spec} R_{s_j}\) the restriction \(\mathcal{F}|_{D(s_j)}\) is, by

4268	  the away localisation \((M_j)_g\) (\texttt{lemma-widetilde-pullback}:
4269	  \(\Gamma(D(g), \widetilde{M}) = M_g\)). Applying this with \(g\) the image of \(f\) shows

4272	  \((M_j) \to (M_j)_f\), i.e.\ the localised map

4274	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for the powers of \(f\).
